coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of polyaniline nanotubes decorated with graphene quantum dots: Structural & electrochemical studies
coredata.dc:creator: Breczko, Joanna
Grzeskiewicz, Barbara
Gradzka, Emilia
Bobrowska, Diana M.
Basa, Anna
Goclon, Jakub
Winkler, Krzysztof
coredata.dc:description: The aim of this paper was to synthesize sulfonated polyaniline nanotubes (s-PANI-NTs) decorated with graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and explore their use as an electrode material in supercapacitors. Spectroscopic (FTIR, Raman, UV–vis, XPS) studies confirmed the presence of both components in the composite, while microscopic (SEM, TEM) analysis proved that the tubular morphological form of PANI was obtained. Moreover, TEM images showed that the decoration of the s-PANI-NT surface with GQDs resulted in the smoothing of the outer part of the polymer wall. Consequently, a higher dispersibility of the s-PANI-NTs/GQDs composite was achieved. A theoretical investigation of the interactions between PANI chains and GQDs was also performed. Furthermore, voltammetric and impedance spectroscopy studies showed that, in the selected concentration range, modification of s-PANI-NTs with GQDs improved the electrochemical properties of the synthesized material. The large BET specific surface area (44.5 m²⋅g−1) and high specific capacitance (245 F⋅g−1) calculated for the optimal s-PANI-NTs/GQDs composite confirmed that the obtained material can be applied in energy storage systems.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The computations were performed on Prometheus supercomputer at ACC Cyfronet AGH-UST. Nitrogen adsorption/desorption analyzer, FTIR spectrometer, SEM, TEM and Raman microscopes as well as the potentiostat system were funded by the European Funds for Regional Development and the National Funds of Ministry of Science and Higher Education as part of the Operational Programme Development of Eastern Poland, projects: POPW.01.03.00–20–034/09–00 and POPW.01.03.00–20–004/11–00.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of polyaniline nanotubes decorated with graphene quantum dots: Structural & electrochemical studies

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Chemistry, University of Bialystok, Ciolkowskiego 1K, 15-245 Bialystok, Poland
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Chemistry, University of Bialystok, Ciolkowskiego 1K, 15-245 Bialystok, Poland

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The aim of this paper was to synthesize sulfonated polyaniline nanotubes (s-PANI-NTs) decorated with graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and explore their use as an electrode material in supercapacitors. Spectroscopic (FTIR, Raman, UV–vis, XPS) studies confirmed the presence of both components in the composite, while microscopic (SEM, TEM) analysis proved that the tubular morphological form of PANI was obtained. Moreover, TEM images showed that the decoration of the s-PANI-NT surface with GQDs resulted in the smoothing of the outer part of the polymer wall. Consequently, a higher dispersibility of the s-PANI-NTs/GQDs composite was achieved. A theoretical investigation of the interactions between PANI chains and GQDs was also performed. Furthermore, voltammetric and impedance spectroscopy studies showed that, in the selected concentration range, modification of s-PANI-NTs with GQDs improved the electrochemical properties of the synthesized material. The large BET specific surface area (44.5 m²⋅g−1) and high specific capacitance (245 F⋅g−1) calculated for the optimal s-PANI-NTs/GQDs composite confirmed that the obtained material can be applied in energy storage systems.

ce:section.ce:para: Supercapacitors have attracted great attention due to their potential applications in different electronic devices and electrical vehicles [1,2]. In these systems, electrical charge can be stored either at the electroactive material/electrolyte solution interface, due to the double layer charging, or in the bulk of electroactive material, which can be oxidized or reduced under electrochemical conditions. In both cases, the capacitance performance is significantly influenced by the morphology, size, porosity, and electrochemically active surface area. Therefore, much attention has been paid to designing and fabrication of new electrode materials with the structure controlled at nanometer level.

Carbonaceous materials, such as carbon nanotubes, carbon nano-onions, graphene, or activated carbon are frequently used as a components of electroactive materials in charge storage devices [3–6]. They offer large surface area, relatively good conductivity and excellent mechanical stability. They can also participate in charge storage process due to the double layer charging. On the one hand, the current response of such structures to potential changes is very fast. On the other hand, the charge is stored only on the surface of the carbon materials, therefore, capacitors based on carbon nanomaterials exhibit high power, but low energy density [7,8].

Pseudocapacitors, such as transition metal oxides and conducting polymers, show much higher specific capacitance due to the faradaic processes that occurs in the bulk of electroactive materials. However, they suffer from poor cyclability which is related to the structural changes of the electroactive materials during charging and discharging processes. Pseudocapacitors also exhibit low rate capability due to the limitations related to the low rate of electron transfer through the layer of electroactive material and, particularly, to the low mass transfer rate during doping and un-doping processes.

In order to overcome limitations of carbon-based double layer capacitors and pseudocapacitors, hybrid materials based on carbon and pseudocapacitive materials are produced [9–11]. Such structures combine properties of both components with synergetic effects. Recently, carbon-based quantum dots have received intense attention as a component of electroactive materials for charge storage devices. These structures can be categorized into two class: carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs). Both of them are composed of graphene sheets a few nm in size. The main difference between GQDs and CQDs results from the arrangement of carbon atoms in their structures. GQDs are formed primarily by sp2 hybridized atoms, while CQDs exhibit an amorphous structure dominated by sp3 hybridization [12,13]. Therefore, GQDs show much more regular 2-D structure and exhibit higher reactivity in comparison to CQDs. The major advantage of carbon-based quantum dots, particularly GQDs, over graphene, carbon nanotubes, carbon nano-onions and other larger carbon nanostructures, is their good dispersibility both in water and in various non-aqueous solvents.

These properties of GQDs as well as marked edge effect allow them for many covalent and non-covalent modifications performed in the solution [14–16]. It was also reported that higher charge can be accumulated at the edges of GQDs in comparison to their basal planes [17]. They also show improved feasibility to combine with other nanomaterials. Regarding the advantages mentioned above, GQDs have been used as components of electroactive materials for capacitors [18–36] and batteries [37–39].

GQDs can significantly contribute to double-layer capacitance as a component of electroactive materials. Incorporation of GQDs into activated carbon layer results in the formation of additional active sites which can accumulate additional charge and improve the rate of charge transfer [18]. Hybrid material of activated carbon and GQDs exhibit tree times higher specific capacitance in comparison to pure activated carbon [19]. A similar effect of double-layer capacitance enhancement was observed for the layer of horizontally aligned carbon nanotubes modified with electrochemically synthesized GQDs [20]. Excellent capacitance behavior was also reported for composite of halloysite nanotubes and GQDs [21]. This composite exhibits specific capacitance in the range of 363 to 216 F⋅g−1 for the 0.5 to 20 A⋅g−1 current density.

Incorporation of GQDs into inorganic or organic pseudocapacitors can also significantly enhance capacitance performance of these materials. This effect can be related to the enhancement of the double-layer component of capacitance, structural modification of the electroactive material or participation of GQDs in the charge transfer processes within the electrode material.

Very promising capacitance properties were reported for heterostructural electrodes based on GQDs and transition metal oxides [22–25]. For example, core-shell Co3O4@GQD composite exhibits specific capacitance of 2435 F⋅g−1 at 1 A⋅g−1 and very good capacitance retention [22]. Excellent capacitance properties were also reported for NiO@Co3O4 hollow spheres decorated with GQDs [23]. Heterostructural MnO2/GQDs nanosheets show specific capacitance of 1100 F⋅g−1 and exhibit high potential window of capacitor performance [24], whereas Fe2O3/GQD composite was tested as a lithium-ion storage material for lithium ion batteries [25].

Study of capacitance performance of GQD/conducting polymer composites were practically limited to the composites containing polypyrrole (PPY) [26–29] and polyaniline (PANI) [30–33]. Capacitance performance of such materials depends on the morphology of polymer component, the method of GQDs preparation and the quantitative composition of composite. In most cases, composites are prepared through in situ polymerization in a GQDs suspension. Li and co-workers [26] reported capacitance as high as 641 F⋅g−1 at 0.2 A⋅g−1 for PPY/GQDs hydrogel composite which is increased by ca. 50% compared to the PPY hydrogel electrode. This effect is related to the hierarchical porous structure of the composite enabling fast ion diffusion during charging and discharging. Composites with GQDs located in the pores of amorphous PPY also exhibit good capacitance performance. Specific capacitance of such composite can reach up to 647 F⋅g−1 and ensures large cycle stability [27]. Capacitance behavior of PPY/GQDs composite can be also improved by incorporation of ferrocenyl moieties onto the GQDs surface [28]. A substantial effect of the amount of GQDs incorporated into the structure of PPY on the capacitance properties of composite was also demonstrated [29].

A significant enhancement of capacitance performance was also reported for composites of GQDs and polyaniline as compared to the pristine PANI [30–33]. A three-component composite of polyacrylonitrile, PANI and GQDs was obtained by solution phase mixing. Such material containing 1.5 wt% of GQDs exhibits capacitance of 589 F⋅g−1 for current density of 670 mA⋅g−1 [30]. Composites of GQDs synthesized through thermal decomposition of graphene and polyaniline nanofibers demonstrated capacitance of 1044 F⋅g−1 at 1 A⋅g−1 in sulfuric acid solution [31]. This material also exhibits relatively good capacitance retention of ca. 80% for 3000 cycles. Amazing enhancement of specific capacitance of 2524 F⋅g−1 at 2 A⋅g−1 can be achieved in the case of composite of PANI and sulfur and nitrogen co-doped GQDs [32]. Excellent capacitance of 3632 F⋅g−1 at 1 A⋅g−1 was reported for GQD/PANI composite [33].

GQDs were applied as an electroactive materials in prototypes of charge storage devices [34–36]. A symmetrical capacitor composed of PPY/GQDs composite and PVA-H3PO4 gel electrolyte exhibits energy density of 83.6 Wh⋅kg−1 at power density of 934.5 W⋅kg−1 [34]. GQDs are particularly useful for formation solid-state flexible supercapacitors. For example, a device composed of GQDs deposited at the carbon cloth surface showed areal capacitance of 70 mF⋅cm−2 [35]. GQDs were also applied for formation of micro-supercapacitor devices. For an asymmetrical Au/GQD/PVA-H3PO4/PANI/Au micro-supercapacitor, capacitance of 0.667 mF⋅cm−2 at 0.015 mA⋅cm−2 was reported [36]. This capacitor also exhibits high rate capability up to 1000 V⋅s−1 and good cycling stability.

In this work, we report for the first time the synthesis of polyaniline nanotubes (PANI-NTs) modified with p-toluenesulfonic acid (TSA) and decorated with GQDs. Modification of PANI-NTs with sulfonic groups (s-PANI-NTs) led to their better separation what in result allowed to maintain their high electrical conductivity and better exposure of the exterior walls for interaction with GQDs. Decorating polymer structures with GQDs significantly increased their dispersibility and porosity, what improved the electrochemical properties of s-PANI-NTs/GQDs composite. The extended theoretical and experimental research, including both the analysis of the interactions between PANI-NTs and GQDs, as well as the physicochemical characterization due to the potential utility of the obtained material in the construction of capacitors, constitute a novelty of the presented work.

All chemicals used in this work were commercially available: citric acid (czda; POCH), aniline (≥ 99.5%; Sigma-Aldrich), p-toluenesulfonic acid (≥ 98%; Sigma-Aldrich), ammonium persulfate (≥ 98%; Sigma), diethyl ether (POCH), dichloromethane (POCH), methanol (POCH), ethanol (96%; POCH), sulfuric acid (96%; POCH), hydrochloric acid (35–38%; POCH) and tetraoctylammonium bromide (≥ 98%; Fluka).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of GQDs, s-PANI-NTs and s-PANI-NTs/GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: GQDs were obtained by pyrolysis of citric acid at 200 °C for 30 min, as reported elsewhere [40]. The average size of obtained GQDs was in the range from 2 to 5 nm. The atomic percentage contribution of oxygen containing functional groups calculated from the XPS peak fitting was found to be ca. 8.3%, 16.36%, and 6.3% for CO, C = O, and COOH, respectively [40]. The synthesis of PANI-NTs modified with TSA acid was carried out according to the procedure described in the literature [41]. All steps of the synthesis of s-PANI-NTs and s-PANI-NTs/GQDs are shown in Scheme 1 . The experimental conditions of this procedure were optimized for the amounts of reagents used. Flasks with 20 mL of aqueous mixtures with different molar concentrations of aniline (0.055 mol⋅dm−3, 0.11 mol⋅dm−3 or 0.165 mol⋅dm−3) and TSA (1.74 mmol⋅dm−3, 3.48 mmol⋅dm−3 or 7.12 mmol⋅dm−3) were placed in an ultrasonic bath for 10 min.

In the meantime, different amounts of ammonium persulfate (APS) were dissolved in 40 mL of deionized water (0.029 mol⋅dm−3, 0.059 mol⋅dm−3 or 0.118 mol⋅dm−3) and cooled to 0 °C. The resulting APS solution was rapidly poured into the mixture containing aniline and TSA and then left for 24 h in an ice bath. The synthesized product was filtered and washed twice with deionized water and once with methanol and diethyl ether. After drying in an oven, a green-black powder was obtained.

The obtained s-PANI-NTs/GQDs composites were analogous to the synthesis of the pristine s-PANI-NTs (Scheme 1). For this purpose, aniline and TSA were dissolved in 20 mL of deionized water (concentration of aniline: 0.055 mol⋅dm−3 and concentration of TSA: 7.12 mmol⋅dm−3), and the flask with the mixture was placed in an ultrasonic bath for 10 min. APS was dissolved in 40 mL of deionized water (0.059 mol⋅dm−3) and cooled to 0 °C. The resulting APS solution was rapidly poured into the mixture containing aniline and TSA and left in an ice bath. In the next step, GQDs were introduced to modify the s-PANI-NT surface. The experimental conditions were optimized for the amount of GQDs and the time at which they were added to the solution with s-PANI-NTs. For this purpose, 5 mL of a GQDs aqueous solution (20 mg⋅mL−1, 40 mg⋅mL−1 or 60 mg⋅mL−1) was added dropwise to the reaction mixture containing the forming nanotubes (4, 12 or 24 h after the introduction of APS). After introducing the GQDs, the mixture was left in the ice bath for an additional 24 h. The synthesized product was filtered and washed twice with deionized water and once with methanol and diethyl ether. After drying in an oven, a green-black powder was obtained.

The SEM images were recorded with the use of an INSPECT S50 microscope (FEI). The accelerating voltage of the electron beam was 15 keV. Nitrogen adsorption/desorption measurements were performed at −196 °C using an ASAP2020 analyzer (Micromeritics Corp.). Before measurements, samples were degassed for 20 h at 130 °C under reduced pressure.

The FTIR spectra were collected at room temperature in the range 400–4000 cm−1 using a Nicolet iN10 MX spectrometer (Thermo Scientific). Raman spectra were recorded with an inVia microscope (Renishaw) using Ar laser at λ=514 nm. UV–vis measurements were carried out on a U-3900H spectrophotometer (Hitachi) in the range 190–800 nm.
The TEM images were recorded on a Tecnai G2 20 X-TWIN microscope (FEI) operated at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV.

The XPS spectra were recorded with a PHI 5000 VersaProbeTM scanning ESCA Microprobe using monochromatic Al-Kα radiation (hv = 1486.6 eV) from an X-ray source operating at 100 µm spot size, 25 W power, and 15 kV acceleration voltage. The high-resolution XPS spectra were collected with the hemispherical analyzer at the pass energy of 23.5 eV, the energy step size of 0.1 eV and the photoelectron take-off angle of 45° with respect to the surface. The measured area was defined as a 250 × 250 μm. The Shirley background subtraction and the peak fitting with Gaussian–Lorentzian-shaped profiles were performed for the high-resolution XPS spectra analysis. For quantification, the PHI Multipak sensitivity factors and determined transmission function of the spectrometer were used.

Voltammetric and faradaic impedance measurements were performed using a PGSTAT 302 N potentiostat (AUTOLAB) with a three-electrode system consisting of Au (ϕ 3 mm) as the working electrode, Pt mesh (0.25 mm) as the counter electrode and Ag/AgCl as the reference electrode. Galvanostatic charge/discharge studies were performed for 7000 cycles using the gold foil (10 × 3 mm) as the working electrode at current density of 7 A⋅g− 1. All these measurements were carried out in 0.1 mol⋅dm3 H2SO4 solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Modification of the gold electrode with s-PANI-NTs and s-PANI-NTs/GQDs films
ce:section.ce:para: In the first step, 3 mg of synthesized material (s-PANI-NTs or s-PANI-NTs/GQDs) was dispersed in 1 mL of TOABr ethanolic solution (10 mmol⋅dm−3). The working gold electrode surface was modified by applying 15 µL of the obtained solution (3 mg⋅mL−1) and evaporating the solvent. TOABr binder was added in order to improve mechanical stability of the composite and prevent GQDs dissolution in water. The binder also provides ionic environment for the electroactive material. Next, the electrode was placed in the aqueous solution containing supporting electrolyte and voltammetric or faradaic impedance measurements were performed.

All simulations were performed using density functional theory (DFT), which is implemented in the Quantum ESPRESSO package in conjunction with ultrasoft pseudopotentials [42]. The exchange-correlation energy was approximated using the generalized gradient approximation (GGA) with the Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE) functional [43]. In addition, Grimme's D3 dispersion corrections with the Becke-Johnson damping function [44] were used to account for the van der Waals interactions. The Kohn-Sham orbitals were expanded using plane waves with an energy cut-off of 30 Ry. The convergence criterion for the electronic self-consistent iteration was set to be lower than 1.4 meV. All the structures were fully relaxed using the Broyden-Fletcher-Goldfarb-Shanno (BFGS) quasi-Newton algorithm until the forces on each atom were less than 0.01 eV/Å [45]. The total energy was minimized using the Hellman-Feynman forces. For the largest system, we applied Brillouin zone sampling at the Γ point. For density of states (DOS) calculations, a 4 × 4 × 1 Monkhorst-Pack k-point mesh was applied [46]. In addition, the peak width for broadening was set to 0.05 eV for better visualization of the DOS. All the calculations were spin polarized.

To characterize the stability of GQDs on the surface of PANI, the adsorption energy (Eads) was calculated, defined as:
(1)

E GQD

)

where EGQD/PANI is the total energy of the mixed system, while EGQD and EPANI denote the total energy of the isolated gas phase GQD and the PANI polymer, respectively. A negative value of Eads means that the adsorption process is energetically favourable.
All figures were produced using the visualization programs VESTA [47] and XcrySDen [48].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the s-PANI-NTs and s-PANI-NTs/GQDs synthesis procedures
ce:section.ce:para: In the optimization process of s-PANI-NTs and s-PANI-NTs/GQDs synthesis procedures, a series of materials were obtained and characterized using SEM microscopy and nitrogen adsorption/desorption techniques. The SEM images recorded for PANI nanostructures synthesized with different aniline contents are shown in Fig. 1 . The polymer material obtained with the highest proportion of aniline (Fig. 1c) did not show the expected structure of nanotubes but exhibited nanoflower form. The use of a lower aniline content in the synthesis resulted in nanotube formation (Fig. 1a-b). However, only for the smallest tested aniline addition (0.055 mol⋅dm−3), the s-PANI-NTs were separated, which was the most advantageous in the context of their further modification with GQDs.

A similar study was performed for a series of materials synthesized with different amounts of TSA (Fig. 1d-f). The SEM images show that the increase in TSA content in the reaction mixture improved the dispersibility of the obtained polymer structures due to their greater degree of modification. The best separation of s-PANI-NTs was obtained for the highest amount of added TSA (7.12 mmol⋅dm−3).

The use of various amounts of APS did not cause any visible morphological changes. However, the introduction of the smallest amount of this oxidant (0.029 mol⋅dm−3) turned out to be insufficient to initiate the polymerization process. Therefore, the minimum amount of this reagent necessary to conduct the polymerization (0.059 mol⋅dm−3) was considered the optimal value of this oxidant.

SEM images recorded for s-PANI-NTs/GQDs composites, which were synthesized by adding the same amount of GQDs (20 mg⋅mL−1) at different stages of polymerization (after 4 h, 12 h or 24 h from APS addition), are shown in Fig. 2 a-c. On their basis, it was concluded that the later GQDs were introduced into the reaction mixture, the more homogeneous material was obtained. The composite formed by adding GQDs 24 h after the start of polymerization (Fig. 2c) showed the most uniform structure. This is probably because GQDs were introduced into the mixture when the s-PANI-NTs had already been well formed and the addition of the carbon component only contributed to their better separation. In other examples (Fig. 2a-b), the GQDs could be partially enclosed in the structure of the s-PANI-NTs, which made it impossible to decorate this polymer form with GQDs.

The influence of the GQD amount on the morphology of the obtained composites was also examined. SEM images recorded for s-PANI-NTs/GQDs materials, which were synthesized by adding various amounts of GQDs (5 mL of 20 mg⋅mL−1, 40 mg⋅mL−1 or 60 mg⋅mL−1 aqueous solution) 24 h after the start of polymerization, are shown in Fig. 2d-f. In the tested GQDs concentration range, it was observed that an increase in the GQD content in the composite also increased the tendency of the synthesized material to form aggregates. The composite obtained by adding the smallest amount of GQDs (20 mg⋅mL−1) (Fig. 2d) showed the best tube separation and was the most homogeneous. In the synthesis of the remaining composites (Fig. 2e-f), an excess amount of GQDs was used, which in turn led to the undesirable effect of nanotubes sticking into larger structures, while the GQDs were expected to only decorate the surface of s-PANI-NTs.

Nitrogen adsorption/desorption measurements allowed to determine the BET specific surface area of ​​each obtained material (Table 1 ). The results suggested that the best sorption properties and the highest specific surface area (35.7 m2⋅g−1) should be found in the polymeric material synthesized with the highest proportion of both aniline and TSA. However, the SEM analysis, described in this subsection, showed that excess of aniline used in the polymerization reaction causes excessive sticking of the polymeric tubes or formation of another morphological form of PANI. It was decisive argument for using the smallest tested amount of aniline in the synthesis procedure.

The data collected in Table 1 also showed that the s-PANI-NTs/GQDs composite, synthesized with the lowest amount of GQDs, which were introduced after the longest time from the start of polymerization, was characterized by the highest BET specific surface area (44.5 m2⋅g−1). The s-PANI-NT and s-PANI-NT/GQDs structures obtained under selected experimental conditions were considered optimal and were subjected to further physicochemical analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spectral characterization of s-PANI-NTs and s-PANI-NTs/GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Spectroscopic characterization was performed to confirm the real structures of the materials obtained through the syntheses. In the FTIR spectrum recorded for GQDs (Fig. 3 ), a characteristic band was observed at 1421 cm−1 corresponding to the stretching vibrations of the C=C bonds. This indicated the existence of a typical graphene structure made of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms. The presence of oxygen groups affecting the dispersibility of GQDs was also confirmed by the presence of bands at 3276, 1712, 1140 and 783 cm−1. They are assigned to the stretching vibrations of OH, C = O, CO and OC–O groups, respectively. In turn, the FTIR spectrum obtained for s-PANI-NTs (Fig. 3) showed a characteristic wide band at 3464 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching vibrations of the NH bond in the PANI structure. Signals at 1586 and 1500 cm−1, corresponding to vibrations of the quinoid and benzoid rings, respectively, also confirmed the existence of a PANI structure in the synthesized material. The peaks at 1309 cm−1 and 1146 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of the CN and C = N bonds, respectively. The presence of sulfonic acid groups related to the modification of PANI nanotubes with TSA was also confirmed by the FTIR method. The bands at 1080, 750 and 630 cm−1 occurring in the recorded spectrum were assigned to the stretching vibrations of the groups O = S = O, S–O and C–S, respectively.

The FTIR spectrum recorded for the s-PANI-NTs/GQDs composite (Fig. 3) contained signals appearing in both the GQD and the s-PANI-NT spectra. For example, the wide double band observed at 3195 and 3064 cm−1 corresponds to the superimposition of the OH bond peak from the GQD spectrum (3276 cm−1) and the NH bond peak assigned to the s-PANI-NT structure (3464 cm−1). Their shift towards smaller wavenumbers, in comparison with the reference materials, suggested the occurrence of interactions between GQDs and s-PANI-NTs. Moreover, the characteristic doublets at 1574 and 1500 cm−1 were attributed to the vibrations in the chinoid and benzoid rings, respectively, while the band at 1722 cm−1 corresponds to the C = O stretching vibrations in the GQD structure. All these signals confirmed the presence of both components in the composite material.

The physicochemical characterization of the obtained material was also performed using Raman spectroscopy. Fig. 4 shows the Raman spectra recorded for GQDs, s-PANI-NTs and s-PANI-NTs/GQDs. As expected, the spectrum recorded for GQDs contained signals typical for carbon nanostructures, namely, D and G bands. The D band at 1352 cm−1 indicated the presence of sp3 hybridized carbon atoms, while the G band, observed at 1604 cm−1, proved the presence of an undisturbed graphene structure with sp2 hybridization. The similar intensities of the D and G bands confirmed a high content of sp3 structural defects. This indicated that oxygen functional groups probably occurred not only at the edges but also inside the GQD plane.

The peaks observed in the Raman spectrum recorded for the s-PANI-NTs confirmed the results from FTIR measurements. In the region of ​​low wavenumbers, two characteristic signals were observed at 575 and 824 cm−1, assigned to NH stretching vibrations and CH deformation vibrations of the chinoid ring, respectively. The band at 1496 cm−1 corresponded to the vibration of C = N stretching, while the signal at 1598 cm−1 confirmed the presence of the stretching vibrations of the C = C bond. The signals observed in the spectrum recorded for the composite come from both of its components. Unfortunately, the peaks related to GQDs (D and G bands) occurred at similar wavenumbers as the signals of s-PANI-NTs (CC and C = C stretching vibration bands at 1353 and 1599 cm−1), leading to their overlapping.

The spectroscopic research was completed with UV–vis measurements (Fig. 5 ). The energy absorption bands of GQDs are closely related to the size and degree of structural modification of the GQDs. The strong band at 215 nm was associated with the π→π* electron transition in C = C bonds, while the signal at 272 nm corresponded to the n→π* transitions. A weak absorption maximum at 320 nm was the result of n→π* transitions within the C = O moiety. This proved the presence of oxygen functional groups in the structure of the obtained GQDs. The observed signals also confirmed the PANI structure. A strong absorption band at 218 nm indicated the transition of π→π* electrons in C = C bonds. The less intense peak at 273 nm corresponded to the n→π* transitions in the C = C moiety. In turn, the absorption maximum at 374 nm was the result of π→π * transitions within the benzoid rings.

Fig. 5 also shows the UV–vis spectra recorded for the selected composite. The high intensity peak at 216 nm, indicating the transition of π→π* electrons in C = C bonds, corresponded to GQDs and confirmed the presence of s-PANI-NTs. Its location was between the values ​​of the wavenumbers assigned to individual components. The wide bands observed at 265 nm and in the range of 320–450 nm (Fig. 5) also proved the presence of both components in the composite. They were assigned to transitions of n→π* in the C = C group, n→π* within the C = O group and π→π * within the benzoid rings, respectively. Changes in the position of the signals in the spectrum of the composite relative to their position in the spectra of the reference materials can be attributed to the GQD vs. s-PANI-NT interactions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microscopic characterization of s-PANI-NTs and s-PANI-NTs/GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the TEM images (Fig. 6 d), it was concluded that the synthesized GQDs exhibited the form of spherical dots with a diameter of a few nanometers (2–5 nm). Moreover, the obtained structures showed a high degree of dispersibility and did not form aggregates. Consistent with the results from TEM measurements (Fig. 6e), SEM images recorded for the polymeric material (Fig. 6a) also confirmed the formation of polyaniline into tubes. The obtained s-PANI-NTs showed a significant tendency to stick together and form aggregates.

Their diameter reached an average of hundred nanometers, while the surface of the walls was clearly fibrous and porous. TEM studies of the composite material (Fig. 6f) confirmed the decoration of polymeric nanotubes with GQDs, which were deposited on the walls of s-PANI-NTs and partially in the cavities. GQDs were not directly visible on the TEM images, but their presence can be indirectly confirmed by the differences in the morphology of the composite tubes in comparison with pristine s-PANI-NTs. The surface of composite walls was smoothed and showed lower porosity than polymer structures without GQDs addition. The reason for this was the penetration of GQDs into the cavities of PANI, which reduced the number of mesopores on the walls of polymeric nanotubes. However, the large amount of introduced GQDs increased the number of micropores, which in total increased the specific surface area of the composite. Moreover, a better separation of the s-PANI-NTs/GQDs was observed in comparison with the pristine polymer. These observations were consistent with the composite morphology analysed by SEM microscopy (Fig. 6b). The spectrum of EDX recorded for s-PANI-NTs/GQDs (Fig. 6c) confirmed the presence of C, O and N in the synthesized material. The appearance of an oxygen peak indicated the presence of GQDs with oxygen-containing groups at the edges of the composite structure.

The analysis of the diameter of pristine s-PANI-NTs and s-PANI-NTs decorated with GQDs, based on TEM images, is shown in Fig. 7 . Histograms showing the statistical distribution of tube diameter (Fig. 7) were made on the basis of more than 100 counts. Initially, s-PANI-NTs were synthesized in the solution containing optimal amounts of aniline and TSA after rapidly adding APS solution and stirring mixture for 24 h in an ice bath. Next, the GQDs were added to the growth solution. After 4, 8 and 24 h of further synthesis, the portions of reaction mixture were taken out, washed and dried. The TEM analysis showed that s-PANI-NTs before and after the addition of the GQDs exhibit significant differences in the diameter. While the most of PANI-NTs exhibit a diameter in the range of 111–130 nm, the dominant values for the composite obtained 4 h after the introduction of GQDs were in the range of 131–150 nm. The tendency for the average diameter to increase is maintained along with the extension of the exposure time to 8 h. After 24 h, a wider range of dominant nanotube diameter is observed. Composite nanotubes with smaller diameter (131–150 nm) and those with larger diameter (171–190 nm) have a similar share in this material. Otherwise, it should be noted that are not many counts for the nanotubes above 200 nm in diameter. However, we must take into account that longer time of interaction between GQDs and PANI also allows for longer polymerization of aniline. Therefore, after addition of GQDs, further increase of s-PANI-NTs diameter is observed. On the one hand, the structure of PANI-NT wall with incorporated GQDs is smoother and more homogeneous (Fig. 6f). On the other hand, due to their small size and good dispersibility, GQDs are not able to significantly change the diameter of the modified polymeric nanotubes. Therefore, the extended polymerization time, and not the addition of GQDs, is responsible for increasing the diameter of the obtained nanotubes. However, GQDs, due to their acidic nature, change the pH of the reaction mixture, which increases the efficiency of the ongoing polymerization process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: XPS analysis of s-PANI-NTs and s-PANI-NTs/GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The materials composition was also estimated by XPS analysis. Fig. 8 shows O 1 s, N 1 s, C 1 s and S 2p XPS spectra obtained for s-PANI-NTs and s-PANI-NTs/GQDs composite. The O 1 s spectra of both materials demonstrate two components at 531.27 eV and 532.5 eV related to the COH/CO and C = O groups, respectively [49]. The N 1 s spectrum is represented by three peaks characteristic for polyaniline. They are derived from –NH- groups and positively charged nitrogen species, =NH+- and N+ [50,51]. In the case of composite, the N 1 s peaks of –NH- and =NH+- shift to higher binding energy and N+to lower energy compared to PANI decorated with TSA. Such behavior suggests the p-π interactions between PANI and graphene layer [52]. These functional groups were also found in C 1 s spectrum. In this case peak centered at 285.6 eV is associated with carbon bonded to the neutral nitrogen atoms CN and C = N. Moreover, this spectrum contains also additional component at 284.5 eV correspond to the neutral CC and CH bonds in the backbone of the polymer. Signals located at 286.24 eV and 287.62 eV are characteristic for carbon connected to the oxygen. Small, high binding energy tail observed at 291 eV is a shakeup satellite peak corresponds to delocalized π conjugation in aromatic structures [53,54]. The C 1 s spectrum of composite material is characterized by the same components as polyaniline decorated with TSA that part of them are sum of components come from polyaniline, TSA and graphene layer. An additional signal located at 288.8 eV corresponds to -COOH edge groups of GQDs [55]. The intensity of CC signal is higher in the case of composite due to increase of the π electron cloud stacking between graphene and aromatic/quinoid structure in PANI, which may facilitate the electron transfer and enhance electrochemical properties of the composite. Moreover, also signals derive from C = O, CO and π electrons are higher in this case due to presence of GQDs in composite material. In addition, the increase of CO signal intensity is associated with the presence of both epoxy and hydroxyl groups in the GQDs. These residua groups may form hydrogen bonds with the polyaniline, and then bring another intimate interaction between graphene and PANI besides the π-π electron stacking [56]. The S 2p spectra can be deconvoluted into two components related to S-OH and S = O groups that confirm the presence of TSA both in polyaniline and polyaniline/GQD composite [57].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Theoretical model for GQD interaction with the surface of PANI
ce:section.ce:para: Periodic boundary conditions and a supercell approximation were applied to model GQD interactions with the surface of PANI. The initial optimization of the unit cell shape of the PANI bulk with the internal atomic coordinates was performed using variable-cell calculations (a = 10.24 Å, b = 5.74 Å, c = 4.46 Å with α = 112.4°, β = 110.5° and γ = 90.5°). Then, we multiplied this cell in the a- and b-directions four and six times, respectively, whereas in the c-direction, an area of approximately 18 Å was placed in the supercell. The PANI supercell containing one layer of the polymer (576 atoms) was fully relaxed. After geometry optimization, every second chain of PANI was rotated by approximately 90–120° to maximize the hydrogen bond interactions between chains.

The initial configuration of the GQD structure was created by taking ca. 20 by 20 Å plate of graphene and saturating it with different oxygen-containing groups at the edges and in the close vicinity of the edges. In our modeling, we referred to the relative concentration of these groups presented in Jeong's et al. paper [14] based on the deconvolution of the high-resolution C1s XPS signal. The GQD model (250 atoms) contains 11 carboxyl groups, 14 hydroxyl groups and 20 carbonyl groups, which is in very good agreement with their relative intensity observed in the experiment (6.38:8.31:16.36, Fig S1) [14]. However, in our model, the relative ratio of carbon to oxygen is higher than that obtained in the experiment (the average value from all GQDs of different sizes). We did not investigate the most stable configurations of GQD since many different configurations can exist.

The optimized structure of GQDs on the surface of PANI is presented in Fig. 9 . The supercell representing the mixed system contains one GQD that is separated by its periodic image by at least 10.5 Å. The calculated Eads is equal to −12.40 eV. The mutual interaction is mainly driven by hydrogen bonds and van der Waals forces with a small contribution of π–π stacking interactions [58]. The shortest distances between the oxygen atoms from the adsorbed GQD and the hydrogen atoms from the PANI polymer are in the range of 2.2 - 3.0 Å.

ce:section.ce:section-title: N2 Adsorption/Desorption characterization of GQDs, s-PANI-NTs and s-PANI-NTs/GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The specific surface area values, pore size and total pore volume distribution of the synthesized GQDs, s-PANI-NTs and s-PANI-NTs/GQDs were determined by nitrogen adsorption/desorption measurements. The recorded nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherms are shown in Fig. 10 . According to the IUPAC classification, GQDs, isotherms obtained for s-PANI-NTs and s-PANI-NTs/GQDs are type IV, which is typical for mesoporous materials and characterized by a sharp increase in the adsorbed volume of N2 and by a hysteresis loop at a high relative pressure (P/P0 = 0.8–1.0) [59,60]. Moreover, the shape of the adsorption hysteresis loop is strictly correlated with pore size and geometry. The recorded isotherms showed noticeable hysteresis type H3 loops, which indicate slit-shaped pores [60].

The Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) specific surface areas are shown in Fig. 11 as linear plots of 1/[Q(P/P0)−1] vs. the relative pressure (P/P0). The BET surface areas were found to be 10.9, 35.3 and 44.5 m2⋅g −1 for GQDs, s-PANI-NTs and s-PANI-NTs/GQDs, respectively (see Table 2 ). A significant increase in the BET value after decorating s-PANI-NT walls with GQDs indicated an improvement in the sorption ability of the composite. Furthermore, in Table 2, other textural data, such as pore size and pore volume calculated by the BJH method, were collected. The pore sizes of s-PANI-NTs/GQDs and reference materials (GQDs and s-PANI-NTs) were between 2.65 and 17.84 nm, which suggested that micropores and mesopores are present in the analysed materials (see Table 2).

The pore volume distribution of GQDs, PANI-NTs and PANI-NTs/GQDs as a function of pore width is presented in Fig. 11. The area under the pore size distribution curves increased after GQDs incorporation into the s-PANI-NT structure, which resulted from an increase in the BET surface area.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical studies of composites containing s-PANI-NTs and GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Large differences in the slope of the peak current – square root of the sweep rate relations obtained for s-PANI-NTs and s-PANI-NTs/GQDs (Fig. 13) reflected the significant difference in the capacitance properties of both materials. Values of specific capacitance, CS, calculated from the integration of voltammograms in the potential range from −0.2 to 0.7 V for composites containing different amounts of GQDs are collected in Table 3 . Initially, the specific capacitance increased with an increase in the amount of GQDs in the synthesized composite and reached a maximum value of 245 F⋅g−1 for the material formed in solution containing ca. 20 mg⋅mL−1 of GQDs. Next, the capacitance started to decrease with an increase in the amount of GQDs in the s-PANI-NTs/GQDs composite. The specific capacitance can be enhanced (305 F⋅g−1) if the potential range of capacitor operation is narrowed (inset in Fig. 13). In this case, the composite exhibit typical capacitance performance. However, the voltammetric profiles depart from the pseudorectangular shape at the potentials of the polarization direction change. A high resistance of the composite material is probably responsible for such behavior. The initial increase in capacitance properties was related to the better separation of s-PANI-NTs decorated with GQDs and to the contribution of oxidized GQDs in the faradaic current. However, the large amount of GQDs within the composite material prevented counterion penetration, leading to a decrease in the capacitance properties of the composite. This effect is probably responsible for relatively high resistance of the composite material.

Quantitative investigation of the capacitance properties of s-PANI-NTs/GQDs composite was performed by galvanostatic charge-discharge measurements. The charge-discharge curves recorded for 7 A⋅g−1 current density are shown in Fig. 14 (inset). The specific capacitance was calculated from the discharge part of these curves according to the following equation:
(2)
C s
= I /
(
d V / d t
)
where I is the applied current density and V is the voltage. The s-PANI-NTs/GQDs composite exhibits specific capacitance of 196 F⋅g−1. This value is consistent with the results of capacitance performance obtained from voltammetric measurements (Fig. 12, Table 3). The s-PANI-NTs/GQDs composite also shows very stable capacitance performance which is almost independent on the number of charge-discharge cycles for 7000 cycles (Fig. 14).

The electrochemical properties of the s-PANI-NTs/GQDs composite were also studied by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy. Fig. 15 a presents the imaginary part of impedance, Z”, as a function of the real part of impedance, Z’, for the electrode covered with s-PANI-NTs/GQDs composite film containing different amounts of GQDs. The Z’ – Z” responses were dominated by the material capacitance. A large increase in the imaginary part of the impedance for low frequencies was observed, particularly for the composite formed in solution containing 20 mg⋅mL−1 GQDs. The composite/electrolyte interphase can be represented by the equivalent circuit shown in Fig. 15 (inset). The first part of this circuit was responsible for the electrode processes at the interphase between the composite material and the solution. Rct is the charge transfer resistance related to polymer electro-oxidation, ZW is the Warburg impedance related to counterions transport accompanying the polymer oxidation process and compensating for the positive charge formed on the composite layer, and Cdl is the double-layer capacitance of the external interphase between the composite and electrolyte. The second part of this circuit represented electrode processes within the micropores of the electroactive material. CL was the capacitance of the internal interphase between the electrode surface and the solution inside the micropores, and RL represented charge transfer resistance within the micropores. The shapes of the experimental impedance plots obtained for composites with different amounts of GQDs were consistent with the curves simulated for the equivalent circuit. The equivalent circuit parameters obtained on the basis of simulations are collected in Table 4 . The increase in charge transfer resistance both at the polymer/electrolyte interphase, Rct, and in the composite pores, RL, with the increase in GQDs amount in the composite was probably related to the inhibition of counterion penetration of polymeric material by GQDs. For composites containing lower amounts of GQDs, the total capacitance of the material was dominated by the double layer capacitance, Cdl. However, the increase of GQDs in the composite material resulted in the increase of CL capacitance participation in the total value of composite capacitance. The changes in total capacitance as a function of frequency are shown in Fig. 16 .

Fig. 15 (inset) shows EIS responses obtained for the composite formed in solution containing 20 mg⋅mL−1 of GQDs, which was the optimal amount of carbon nanomaterial for the best capacitance performance. At high frequencies, the impedance responses exhibited a semicircle related to polyaniline oxidation. The Rct values corresponding to the electrooxidation of polyaniline the lower, the more positive is the potential applied to the electrode. Such dependence of charge transfer resistance on the potential was predicted for electron exchange processes at the electrode/conducting polymer interface in low overpotential range [61].

As PANI-NTs decorated with GQDs are considered potential future candidates for capacitors and materials for battery construction, it is a basic requirement to know their electronic properties. Fig. 17 shows the total and projected DOS for the PANI/GQD system calculated at the DFT(PBE)+D3(BJ) level of theory. The system exhibits metallic character, where the unoccupied band just above the Fermi level corresponds to both PANI and GQD states, while the next unoccupied bands located in the range 0.3 to 1.8 eV originate only from GQDs. Therefore, GQDs also contribute to the increase in the current in the potential range more positively than the potentials of the first polyaniline oxidation step. In addition, we also utilized the charge density difference (Δρ(r)) to show the electron transfer from the PANI polymer to GQDs after adsorption. In Fig. 17, the yellow color represents regions of excess electron charge, while the blue color corresponds to deficient regions. The strong intensity of the yellow and blue lobes (isovalue of 8∙10−4 e/Å3) reveals the direction of the electron transfer, that is, from PANI to the oxygen-containing groups of GQD. It is thus clear that the adsorption of GQDs on PANI strongly modified the electronic properties of PANI such that the oxidation of such a mixed system is much easier than the polymer itself.

The aim of this work was to optimize the synthesis conditions to obtain well-separated polyaniline nanotubes modified with p-toluenesulfonic acid and decorated with graphene quantum dots. The electroactive material is composed of polyaniline nanotubes with GQDs incorporated into the structure of their walls. Results of XPS studies indicate the p-π and hydrogen bonding interaction between PANI and GQDs facilitating the charge transfer within the composite material. The obtained composite based on GQD-decorated polyaniline nanotubes shows promising dispersibility and high homogeneity. From the nitrogen adsorption/desorption measurements for the selected composite, the size of its specific surface BET (44.5 m2⋅g−1), the size of the pores (17.8 nm) and the distribution of pores in the material were determined. The large surface area of the synthesized composite and electrochemical activity of its polymeric component make this material particularly useful for charge storage devices.

Polyaniline has been frequently used as an electroactive material in supercapacitors. The specific capacitance of chemically polymerized aniline is in the range of 150–400 F⋅g−1. Capacitance properties can be significantly increased through incorporation of carbon nanomaterials in the structure of polyaniline. Selected values of specific capacitances reported for compositions of polyaniline and GQDs in the literature are summarized in Table 5 . In the same table, the results obtained for the s-PANI-NTs/GQDs composite in this work are also presented. The capacitance properties of s-PANI-NTs/GQDs depend on the composite composition. The highest value of 245 F⋅g−1 was obtained for the composite, which was formed in solution containing aniline (0.055 mol⋅dm−3), TSA (7.12 mmol⋅dm−3), APS (0.059 mol⋅dm−3) and GQDs (20 mg⋅mL−1). This value is more than two times higher than the specific capacitance of pristine s-PANI-NTs.

The literature data presented in Table 5 show even higher capacitances of PANI/GQDs composites [31–33]. These values were obtained for PANI nanofibers [31,32] or spherical PANI nanoparticles [33]. However, it is surprising that the capacitances of PANI/GQDs composites deposited on nickel foam are few times higher than the maximal theoretical value of pristine polyaniline specific capacitance, which is estimated to be 750 F⋅g−1 at 50% doping [62] and significant contribution of Ni oxidation to the total capacitance was not reported in this case [33].

Theoretical modeling of the interphase between PANI and GQDs indicates that carbon nanostructures can participate in electron transfer during PANI electrooxidation. The oxidation of the composite is much easier than that of the polymer itself. A significant shift of PANI oxidation potential towards less positive values is observed for s-PANI-NTs decorated with GQDs in comparison to the potential of the film composed of only s-PANI-NTs .
